metadata.notes: Batch 2 focuses on grammar patterns: modal verbs, subordinate clause word order, question words, and civic/official vocabulary critical for inburgering exam

exercises.question: Which modal verb expresses OBLIGATION? Ik ______ morgen naar de dokter.
exercises.explanation: Moeten = must / have to (obligation). Willen = to want, mogen = to be allowed, kunnen = to be able to. 'Ik moet naar de dokter' = I have to go to the doctor.

exercises.question: Which modal verb expresses PERMISSION? ______ ik hier roken?
exercises.explanation: Mogen = to be allowed / may. 'Mag ik...?' = May I...? From the corpus and vocabulary: '*Mag ik...? = May I...?' Essential for polite requests.

exercises.question: Which modal verb expresses ABILITY? ______ jij goed zwemmen?
exercises.explanation: Kunnen = to be able to / can. 'Kun jij zwemmen?' = Can you swim? Note: kunnen has two forms: kan (ik/hij/zij) and kun (jij/informal). From corpus: 'Kun jij...?'
exercises.grammar_focus: modal verbs: kunnen (ability) — kan vs kun

exercises.question: Which modal verb expresses DESIRE/WANT? Ik ______ graag een kopje koffie.
exercises.explanation: Willen = to want. 'Ik wil graag...' = I would like... (polite form with 'graag'). From corpus: 'Wil je koffie of thee?' Note: wil + graag is very polite.
exercises.grammar_focus: modal verbs: willen (desire) — wil + graag = I would like

exercises.question: Choose the correct modal: Je ______ niet te betalen. Het is gratis. (You don't need to pay.)
exercises.explanation: Hoeven niet = don't need to (absence of obligation). CRITICAL: 'Moet niet' = must NOT (prohibition). 'Hoeft niet' = don't have to. From vocabulary: 'Je hoeft ze niet de hele dag te verzorgen.' This is a very common A2 mistake.
exercises.grammar_focus: modal verbs: hoeven niet (don't need to) vs moet niet (must not) — CRITICAL DISTINCTION

exercises.question: Find the error: 'Je moet niet een jas aantrekken. Het is warm.' (You don't need to put on a coat. It's warm.)

options.A: No error — the sentence is correct
options.B: Should be: 'Je hoeft geen jas aan te trekken'
options.C: Should be: 'Je mag niet een jas aantrekken'
options.D: Should be: 'Je wil niet een jas aantrekken'

exercises.explanation: 'Moet niet' means you MUST NOT (prohibition). For 'don't need to', use 'hoeven niet'. Correct: 'Je hoeft geen jas aan te trekken.' This is one of the most common A2 grammar errors.
exercises.grammar_focus: error: moet niet (prohibition) vs hoeft niet (no obligation)

exercises.question: Complete the polite request: ______ ik even uw paspoort zien?
exercises.explanation: 'Mag ik...?' = May I...? This is the standard polite request form in Dutch. From vocabulary: '*Mag ik...? = May I...?' Used in official contexts: shops, offices, government.

exercises.question: Which sentence correctly expresses 'I don't feel like it'?
exercises.explanation: 'Geen zin hebben' = to not feel like it / to have no desire. From the corpus: 'Je moet het niet doen, als je geen zin hebt.' This is a fixed expression. 'Ik wil niet' = I don't want to (stronger).
exercises.grammar_focus: fixed expression: geen zin hebben (to not feel like)

exercises.question: Choose the sentence with CORRECT Dutch word order: subordinate clause with 'omdat'.

options.A: Ik ga niet, omdat ik ben ziek.
options.B: Ik ga niet, omdat ik ziek ben.
options.C: Ik ga niet, ik ben ziek omdat.
options.D: Ik ga niet, omdat ben ik ziek.

exercises.explanation: In subordinate clauses (after omdat, want, als, dat, hoewel, etc.), the VERB goes to the END. 'omdat ik ziek BEN' — the verb 'ben' moves to the final position. This is the most tested A2 grammar rule.
exercises.grammar_focus: word order: verb-final in subordinate clauses (SOV)

exercises.question: Choose the correct word order: 'I know that he works in Amsterdam.'

options.A: Ik weet dat hij werkt in Amsterdam.
options.B: Ik weet dat hij in Amsterdam werkt.
options.C: Ik weet dat in Amsterdam hij werkt.
options.D: Ik weet hij in Amsterdam werkt.

exercises.explanation: After 'dat' (that), the verb moves to the END: 'dat hij in Amsterdam WERKT.' The verb always goes last in a subordinate clause: dat + subject + rest + VERB.

exercises.question: Choose the correct word order with a modal verb in a subordinate clause: 'because I have to work'

options.C: Both A and B are correct

exercises.explanation: With modal + infinitive in a subordinate clause, BOTH orders are accepted: 'moet werken' (modal → infinitive) and 'werken moet' (infinitive → modal). 'Omdat ik moet werken' is more common in speech. Both are correct.
exercises.grammar_focus: word order: modal + infinitive in subordinate clause — both orders accepted

exercises.question: A time expression starts the sentence. Choose the correct order: 'Tomorrow I am going to the doctor.'

options.A: Morgen ik ga naar de dokter.
options.B: Morgen ga ik naar de dokter.
options.C: Morgen ik naar de dokter ga.
options.D: Ik ga morgen naar de dokter is also correct

exercises.explanation: The V2 rule (verb second): when a time expression like 'morgen' starts the sentence, the verb must be in position 2, so subject and verb INVERT: 'Morgen GA IK...' This is fundamental Dutch word order.
exercises.grammar_focus: word order: V2 rule — inversion after fronted time expressions

exercises.question: Which sentence is correct? (Question with inversion)
exercises.explanation: In questions, the verb comes before the subject (inversion): question word + VERB + subject + rest. 'Wanneer KUN JIJ komen?' Note: 'jij' after the verb → 'kun jij' (not 'kan jij').
exercises.grammar_focus: word order: questions — question word + verb + subject

exercises.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'ook' (also)? Context: You want to say 'I also like coffee.'
exercises.explanation: 'Ook' (also) typically follows the verb: 'Ik wil OOK koffie.' Option C ('Ook ik...') is also grammatically possible but emphasises that YOU ALSO want coffee (contrastive). Option B is the natural, neutral placement.
exercises.grammar_focus: word order: ook (also) — placement after the verb

exercises.question: Complete with correct word order: 'Ik weet niet ______ hij morgen komt.'
exercises.explanation: 'Of' introduces indirect yes/no questions: 'Ik weet niet OF hij morgen komt.' = I don't know WHETHER he's coming tomorrow. 'Dat' introduces a statement ('Ik weet dat hij komt' = I know that he's coming).

exercises.question: Which question word asks about METHOD or WAY? '______ kom je naar je werk?'
exercises.explanation: Hoe = how. 'Hoe kom je naar je werk?' = How do you get to work? From the corpus: 'Hoe kom je naar je werk?' Hoe is used for manner, method, and condition.

exercises.question: Which question word asks about LOCATION? '______ woon je?'
exercises.explanation: Waar = where. 'Waar woon je?' = Where do you live? From the corpus: 'Waar woon je?' Waar asks about place/location.

exercises.question: Which question word asks about REASON? '______ leer je Nederlands?'
exercises.explanation: Waarom = why. 'Waarom leer je Nederlands?' = Why are you learning Dutch? From the corpus: 'Waarom?' Waarom always asks for a reason or explanation.

exercises.question: Which question asks about DURATION? '______ doe je dat al?'
exercises.explanation: Hoe lang = how long (duration). 'Hoe lang doe je dat al?' = How long have you been doing that? From the corpus: 'Hoe lang doe je dat al?' Don't confuse with 'hoe laat' (what time).
exercises.grammar_focus: question word: hoe lang (duration) vs hoe laat (time)

exercises.question: What is the difference between 'Hoe laat?' and 'Hoe lang?'

options.A: No difference — both ask about time
options.B: Hoe laat = what time (clock); Hoe lang = how long (duration)
options.C: Hoe laat = how late; Hoe lang = how tall
options.D: Hoe laat is formal; Hoe lang is informal

exercises.explanation: Hoe laat = what time (o'clock). 'Hoe laat is het?' = What time is it? Hoe lang = how long (duration). 'Hoe lang duurt het?' = How long does it take? Both appear frequently in the corpus.
exercises.grammar_focus: confusing question words: hoe laat vs hoe lang

exercises.question: Which question asks about FREQUENCY? '______ ga je naar de sportschool?'
exercises.explanation: Hoe vaak = how often. 'Hoe vaak ga je naar de sportschool?' = How often do you go to the gym? From the corpus: 'Hoe vaak heb je les?' Typical answers: elke dag, een keer per week, twee keer per maand.
exercises.grammar_focus: question word: hoe vaak (how often — frequency)

exercises.question: Which question asks about QUANTITY/NUMBER? '______ kamers heb je?'
exercises.explanation: Hoeveel = how many / how much. 'Hoeveel kamers heb je?' = How many rooms do you have? From the corpus: 'Hoeveel kamers heb je?' Hoeveel works for both countable and uncountable.

exercises.question: Complete: '______ ben je jarig?' — asking for the date of someone's birthday.
exercises.explanation: Wanneer = when. 'Wanneer ben je jarig?' = When is your birthday? From the corpus. Note: 'jarig zijn' = to have one's birthday (today). 'Ik ben jarig' = It's my birthday.

exercises.question: How do you say 'How can I reach you?' in Dutch?
exercises.explanation: From the corpus: 'Hoe kan ik je bereiken?' = How can I reach you? Hoe = how (method). Bereiken = to reach/contact. This is a common practical phrase.
exercises.grammar_focus: question pattern: Hoe + kan + subject + infinitive?

exercises.question: How do you say 'What did you do at Christmas?' in Dutch?

options.B: Wat heb je met Kerst gedaan?

exercises.explanation: From the corpus: 'Wat heb je met Kerst gedaan?' = What did you do at Christmas? This uses the perfect tense: heb + gedaan (past participle of doen). Met Kerst = at Christmas.
exercises.grammar_focus: perfect tense: heb + gedaan; met Kerst (at Christmas)

exercises.question: How do you say 'I don't feel well' in Dutch?

options.C: Both A and B are correct

exercises.explanation: Both 'Ik voel me niet goed' and 'Ik voel me niet lekker' are correct and natural. From corpus: 'Ik voel me niet lekker.' The reflexive verb 'zich voelen' is key here.

exercises.question: How do you say 'We are looking for something bigger' in Dutch?

options.D: Both B and C are correct

exercises.explanation: From the corpus: 'We zoeken iets groters.' = We're looking for something bigger. 'iets + comparative + -s' is a set pattern. 'We zoeken een groter huis' is also correct and more specific.
exercises.grammar_focus: comparison with 'iets': iets + comparative + -s (iets groters, iets beters)

exercises.question: Choose the correct conjunction: 'I can't come ______ I have to work.' (reason/cause)
exercises.explanation: Want = because (gives a reason). 'Ik kan niet komen, want ik moet werken.' From vocabulary: '*Ik kan niet, want...* = I can't, because...' After 'want', normal word order continues (unlike 'omdat').
exercises.grammar_focus: coordinating conjunction: want (because) — normal word order

exercises.question: Choose the correct conjunction: 'I'm tired, ______ I'll go to bed early.' (result/conclusion)
exercises.explanation: Dus = so / therefore (result). 'Ik ben moe, dus ik ga vroeg naar bed.' = I'm tired, so I'll go to bed early. Dus introduces a consequence. Normal word order after dus.

exercises.question: Choose the correct conjunction: 'I like tea ______ I don't like coffee.' (contrast)
exercises.explanation: Maar = but (contrast). 'Ik vind thee lekker, maar ik vind koffie niet lekker.' = I like tea but I don't like coffee. Maar introduces a contrasting idea.

exercises.question: Find the error in word order: 'Ik ga niet mee, omdat ik moet morgen werken.'

options.B: Should be: 'omdat ik morgen moet werken'
options.C: Should be: 'omdat morgen ik werken moet'
options.D: Should be: 'want ik moet morgen werken'

exercises.explanation: In a subordinate clause (after omdat), the verb goes to the END. 'Morgen' is a time expression that comes before the verb. Correct: 'omdat ik morgen moet werken.' Time expressions: subject + time + verb (at end).
exercises.grammar_focus: word order error: subordinate clause — time expression before verb

exercises.question: What is the key grammatical difference between 'want' and 'omdat'?

options.A: Want is formal, omdat is informal
options.B: Want = normal word order; omdat = verb goes to end
options.C: Omdat = normal word order; want = verb goes to end
options.D: No grammatical difference — they are fully interchangeable

exercises.explanation: CRITICAL RULE: 'Want' (coordinating) = normal word order. 'Omdat' (subordinating) = verb moves to end. 'Ik ben ziek, WANT ik voel me niet lekker.' vs 'Ik blijf thuis OMDAT ik me niet lekker voel.'
exercises.grammar_focus: CRITICAL: want (normal order) vs omdat (verb-final) — most tested A2 distinction

exercises.question: Complete with the correct word order after 'als': 'Bel me, als je ______ tijd ______.'
exercises.explanation: After 'als' (if/when), it's a subordinate clause → verb goes to the end: 'als je tijd HEBT.' = if you have time. From corpus: 'Goed idee! Wanneer kun je?' Pattern: als + subject + rest + VERB.

exercises.question: What does 'inburgeren' mean?

options.B: to integrate into Dutch society / civic integration

exercises.explanation: Inburgeren = to integrate into Dutch society. Het inburgeringexamen = the civic integration exam. This is the core concept of the whole course — knowing this word is essential!

exercises.question: What does 'gemeente' mean?
exercises.explanation: De gemeente = municipality/local council. This is where you register when you move to the Netherlands (inschrijven bij de gemeente). Very important for inburgering.

exercises.question: What does 'inschrijven' mean in a civic context?
exercises.explanation: Inschrijven = to register (officially). 'Je moet je inschrijven bij de gemeente' = You must register at the municipality. Also: inschrijven op een cursus (enrol on a course). Separable verb: in + schrijven.

exercises.question: What is the correct article for 'gemeente' (municipality)?
exercises.explanation: De gemeente. Most governmental and administrative institutions use 'de': de gemeente, de overheid, de politie, de rechtbank.

exercises.question: What does 'aanvragen' mean?
exercises.explanation: Aanvragen = to apply for / to request (official). 'Een vergunning aanvragen' = to apply for a permit. Separable verb: aan + vragen. Common in official Dutch contexts.

exercises.question: What does 'vergunning' mean?
exercises.explanation: De vergunning = permit / licence (official). Work permit = werkvergunning. Building permit = bouwvergunning. Important civic vocabulary.

exercises.question: What is the correct article for 'vergunning' (permit)?
exercises.explanation: De vergunning. The -ing rule: ALL nouns ending in -ing are 'de'. De vergunning, de opleiding, de woning, de verzekering — they all follow this rule.
exercises.grammar_focus: de vs het – suffix rule: -ing = always 'de'

exercises.question: What does 'overheid' mean?
exercises.explanation: De overheid = the government / public authorities (general term). Different from regering (cabinet/administration) or parlement (parliament). Important for civic knowledge.

exercises.question: What does 'belasting' mean?
exercises.explanation: De belasting = tax. Belastingdienst = Tax Authority (Dutch IRS). Inkomstenbelasting = income tax. Important civic and financial vocabulary.

exercises.question: What does 'uitkering' mean?
exercises.explanation: De uitkering = state benefit / welfare payment (e.g., unemployment benefit). From uit + kering (payment out). Important for understanding the Dutch social system.

exercises.question: What is the correct article for 'uitkering' (benefit)?
exercises.explanation: De uitkering. -ing rule again — never fails! All -ing nouns are 'de': de uitkering, de beloning, de vergadering, de opleiding.
exercises.grammar_focus: de vs het – -ing suffix rule reinforcement

exercises.question: What does 'DigiD' refer to in the Netherlands?

options.A: A digital identity / login system for government services
options.B: A type of digital bank card

exercises.explanation: DigiD = Digitale Identiteit. It's the digital login system used to access Dutch government services online (belasting, zorgverzekering, gemeente). Essential for living in the Netherlands.
exercises.grammar_focus: civic vocabulary: DigiD (digital identity for government)

exercises.question: What does 'solliciteren' mean?
exercises.explanation: Solliciteren = to apply for a job. De sollicitatie = the job application. De sollicitatiebrief = cover letter. Solliciteren bij een bedrijf = to apply to a company.
exercises.grammar_focus: work vocabulary: solliciteren (to apply for a job)

exercises.question: What does 'cv' (curriculum vitae) refer to in Dutch?
exercises.explanation: CV = curriculum vitae (résumé). Het cv. In the Netherlands, a standard job application includes a cv + sollicitatiebrief (cover letter). Important for work vocabulary.

exercises.question: What does 'contract' mean in a work context?
exercises.explanation: Het contract = contract / agreement. Arbeidscontract = employment contract. Vast contract = permanent contract. Tijdelijk contract = temporary contract. Key work vocabulary.
exercises.grammar_focus: work vocabulary: het contract – types of employment

exercises.question: What is the difference between 'vast contract' and 'tijdelijk contract'?

options.A: No difference — they are the same
options.B: Vast = permanent contract; tijdelijk = temporary/fixed-term contract
options.D: Vast is for employees; tijdelijk is for self-employed

exercises.explanation: Vast contract = permanent employment contract (no end date). Tijdelijk contract = temporary/fixed-term contract (has an end date). Very important distinction for the Dutch job market.

exercises.question: What does 'loon / salaris' mean?
exercises.explanation: Loon / salaris = salary / wages. Het loon (wages, often hourly). Het salaris (monthly salary). Both are used. Minimum loon = minimum wage (important civic concept).
exercises.grammar_focus: work vocabulary: het loon / het salaris

exercises.question: What does 'bang zijn voor' mean?
exercises.explanation: Bang zijn voor = to be afraid of. 'Ik ben bang voor honden' = I'm afraid of dogs. The preposition is 'voor': bang VOOR iets/iemand.

exercises.question: What does 'zich zorgen maken over' mean?
exercises.explanation: Zich zorgen maken over = to worry about. 'Ik maak me zorgen over mijn gezondheid.' = I'm worried about my health. Reflexive + over. Important for expressing concerns.
exercises.grammar_focus: reflexive expression: zich zorgen maken over (to worry about)

exercises.question: What does 'teleurgesteld zijn' mean?
exercises.explanation: Teleurgesteld = disappointed. 'Ik ben teleurgesteld over het resultaat.' De teleurstelling = the disappointment. Common emotion vocabulary for A2.

exercises.question: What does 'gelukkig' mean?
exercises.explanation: Gelukkig = happy (adjective: 'Ik ben gelukkig') AND luckily/fortunately (adverb: 'Gelukkig is alles goed'). From corpus: 'Gelukkig! Ik hou niet van kou.' = Luckily! I don't like cold. Context is key.

exercises.question: What does 'helaas' mean?
exercises.explanation: Helaas = unfortunately. From the corpus: 'Helaas niet. Ik moet werken tot 17 uur.' = Unfortunately not. I have to work until 5pm. Very common in polite Dutch refusals.

exercises.question: What does 'hopen' mean?
exercises.explanation: Hopen = to hope. 'Ik hoop dat...' = I hope that... From the corpus: 'Dank je. Ik hoop dat het snel droog wordt.' = I hope it dries up soon. Hopen + dat + subordinate clause.

exercises.question: Choose the correct negation: 'I have ______ car.' (I don't have a car.)
exercises.explanation: RULE: Geen is used to negate nouns (no + noun). 'Ik heb geen auto' = I have no car / I don't have a car. 'Niet' negates verbs, adjectives, and specific elements — but NOT 'een + noun'.
exercises.grammar_focus: negation: geen (no + noun) vs niet (not + verb/adj)

exercises.question: Choose the correct negation: 'That is ______ good.' (That is not good.)
exercises.explanation: RULE: Niet is used to negate adjectives, verbs, and adverbs. 'Dat is NIET goed.' Geen is only used before nouns (geen auto, geen tijd, geen probleem).

exercises.question: Choose the correct negation: 'I have ______ time.' (I don't have any time.)
exercises.explanation: 'Geen tijd' = no time. Geen negates the noun 'tijd'. From corpus: 'dat je geen tijd hebt.' RULE: geen replaces 'een' or is used where there's no article before the noun.
exercises.grammar_focus: negation: geen + noun (geen tijd, geen zin, geen geld)

exercises.question: Which sentence uses 'geen probleem' correctly?
exercises.explanation: 'Geen probleem' = no problem. From corpus: 'Geen probleem, ik wacht wel.' A very common fixed expression. Geen + noun (probleem has no article here → use geen).
exercises.grammar_focus: fixed expression: geen probleem + geen before nouns without article

exercises.question: What does 'over een paar jaar' mean?
exercises.explanation: 'Over' + time = in (future). 'Over een paar jaar' = in a few years. From corpus: 'Misschien, over een paar jaar.' Compare: 'een paar jaar geleden' = a few years ago (past).
exercises.grammar_focus: time expression: over + time (future) — over een jaar, over twee weken

exercises.question: What does 'een maand geleden' mean?
exercises.explanation: Geleden = ago (past). 'Een maand geleden' = a month ago. From corpus: 'Hij heeft me een maand geleden al een keer gevraagd.' Pattern: time + geleden = time ago.

exercises.question: What does 'al vijf jaar' mean in 'Ik doe dit al vijf jaar'?

options.B: already for five years / five years now

exercises.explanation: 'Al' + time = already for... / for [time] now (ongoing). From corpus: 'Al vijf jaar. Ik oefen elke dag.' = For five years now. Also: 'Hoe lang doe je dat al?' = How long have you been doing that?

exercises.question: Choose the correct time word: 'I have ______ been waiting for an hour.' (still waiting, ongoing)
exercises.explanation: Nog = still (ongoing). 'Ik wacht nog een uur.' = I am still waiting. Al = already. Pas = just/only (recently or surprisingly short time). Nog implies something is continuing.
exercises.grammar_focus: time words: nog (still) vs al (already) vs pas (just/only)

exercises.question: Choose: 'The shop is ______ open.' (It's already open — earlier than expected.)
exercises.explanation: Al = already. 'De winkel is al open.' = The shop is already open. Al implies something happened sooner than expected or at this point. Nog = still (it hasn't stopped). Pas = just/only.

exercises.question: What does 'tot' mean in 'Ik werk tot 17 uur'?
exercises.explanation: Tot = until. 'Ik werk tot 17 uur' = I work until 5pm. From corpus: 'Helaas niet. Ik moet werken tot 17 uur.' Also: 'Ja, tot 18 uur.' = Yes, until 6pm.

exercises.question: What does 'sinds' mean?

options.B: since (from a point in the past)

exercises.explanation: Sinds = since (from a past point continuing to now). 'Ik woon hier sinds 2019.' = I have lived here since 2019. From vocabulary: '*Sinds wanneer...? = Since when...?' Used with present tense in Dutch (unlike English past perfect).
exercises.grammar_focus: preposition: sinds (since) — used with present tense in Dutch

exercises.question: Find the error: 'Ik woon hier sinds drie jaar.' (I have lived here for three years.)

options.A: No error — this is correct Dutch
options.B: Should use 'al drie jaar': 'Ik woon hier al drie jaar'
options.C: Should use 'voor': 'Ik woon hier voor drie jaar'
options.D: Should use past tense: 'Ik heb hier woonde drie jaar'

exercises.explanation: For a duration (for X years), Dutch uses 'al': 'Ik woon hier al drie jaar.' 'Sinds' is used with a specific point in time: 'Ik woon hier sinds 2021.' This is a common learner error.
exercises.grammar_focus: error: sinds (since a point) vs al + duration (for X time)

exercises.question: What does 'koopje' mean?
exercises.explanation: Het koopje = a bargain / good deal. From the vocabulary list. Related to kopen (to buy) + diminutive -je. 'Dat is een koopje!' = That's a bargain!

exercises.question: What does 'toename' mean?
exercises.explanation: De toename = increase / rise. Toe = to/towards + naam/nemen (to take). Often used in civic/social contexts: 'een toename van het aantal gebruikers' = an increase in the number of users.

exercises.question: What does 'gebruiker' mean?
exercises.explanation: De gebruiker = user (of a product/service/platform). From vocabulary. Gebruiken = to use → gebruiker = user. Important for technology and social media vocabulary.

exercises.question: What does 'communiceren' mean?
exercises.explanation: Communiceren = to communicate. De communicatie = communication. Used in work and social contexts. 'Hoe communiceert u met collega's?' = How do you communicate with colleagues?

exercises.question: What does 'profiel' mean in a digital context?
exercises.explanation: Het profiel = profile (on social media, job websites, etc.). 'Je LinkedIn-profiel' = your LinkedIn profile. Words ending in -iel are typically 'het': het profiel, het materiaal... wait: het profiel.

exercises.question: Complete: Voor werk gebruik ik LinkedIn, maar ______ gebruik ik liever WhatsApp.
exercises.explanation: Privé = private / in private life (as opposed to work). From corpus: 'Voor werk wel, privé gebruik ik liever WhatsApp.' Privé is a common A2 word for distinguishing work and personal life.

exercises.question: What does 'huizenprijzen' mean?
exercises.explanation: Huizenprijzen = house prices. Huis (plural: huizen) + prijzen (prices). From corpus: 'Ja, de huizenprijzen zijn hoog.' = Yes, house prices are high. Compound plural noun.

exercises.question: What does 'aan zee' mean?

options.C: by the sea / on the coast

exercises.explanation: Aan zee = by the sea / on the coast. From corpus: 'We huren een huis aan zee.' = We're renting a house by the sea. 'Aan' is used for location next to water: aan zee, aan de rivier.

exercises.question: What does 'het strand' mean?
exercises.explanation: Het strand = the beach. From corpus: 'Heb je zin om zaterdag mee te gaan naar het strand?' Note: het strand (neuter article). Very common leisure destination in the Netherlands.

exercises.question: Complete: Heb je zin om zaterdag mee te gaan ______ het strand?
exercises.explanation: Naar = to (direction/destination). 'Mee te gaan naar het strand' = to come along to the beach. With destinations, always 'naar': naar school, naar het werk, naar de dokter.

exercises.question: What is 'tomatensaus'?
exercises.explanation: Tomatensaus = tomato sauce. Tomaten (tomatoes) + saus (sauce). From corpus: 'Tomatensaus met groenten.' = Tomato sauce with vegetables. Useful food vocabulary.

exercises.question: What is the correct article for 'saus' (sauce)?
exercises.explanation: De saus. Most food condiments and liquid accompaniments use 'de': de saus, de soep, de dressing.

exercises.question: What does 'zender' mean?
exercises.explanation: De zender = TV channel / broadcasting station. From vocabulary. Also: zenden = to broadcast/send. 'Op welke zender is dat?' = On which channel is that?

exercises.question: What does 'terugkijken' mean?
exercises.explanation: Terugkijken = to watch back / catch up on TV. From vocabulary. Separable: terug + kijken. 'Je kunt het terugkijken op internet.' = You can watch it back online. Also: terugkijken op = to look back on (reflect).
exercises.grammar_focus: media vocabulary: terugkijken (watch back / catch up)

exercises.question: What is the difference between 'evenement' and 'gebeurtenis'?

options.B: Evenement = planned event/happening; gebeurtenis = occurrence/event (often unexpected)
options.C: Evenement is formal; gebeurtenis is informal
options.D: Gebeurtenis is only for negative events

exercises.explanation: Het evenement = a planned event (festival, concert, sports event). De gebeurtenis = an occurrence/event (often historical or unexpected). 'Het festival is een groot evenement.' vs 'De val van de Berlijnse Muur was een historische gebeurtenis.'

exercises.question: What does 'diploma-uitreiking' mean?
exercises.explanation: Diploma-uitreiking = graduation ceremony. Diploma + uitreiking (distribution/handing out). 'De diploma-uitreiking is volgende week.' = The graduation ceremony is next week.

exercises.question: You have moved to a new city in the Netherlands. What is the FIRST thing you must do officially?

options.C: Register at the gemeente (inschrijven bij de gemeente)

exercises.explanation: You must inschrijven bij de gemeente (register at the municipality). This gives you a BSN (burger service nummer), which you need for everything else: DigiD, bank account, health insurance, tax. Critical civic knowledge.
exercises.grammar_focus: civic procedure: inschrijven bij de gemeente — first step

exercises.question: What is a 'BSN'?

options.B: Burger Service Nummer — citizen service number (like a national ID number)

exercises.explanation: BSN = Burger Service Nummer (Citizen Service Number). It's your personal identification number in the Netherlands, used for taxes, healthcare, government services. You get it when you register at the gemeente.

exercises.question: What does 'eigen risico' mean in the context of Dutch healthcare?

options.B: Compulsory own-risk deductible (amount you pay yourself per year)

exercises.explanation: Eigen risico = compulsory deductible. Every Dutch resident with zorgverzekering pays the first €385 (approx.) of healthcare costs themselves each year. Essential for understanding Dutch health insurance.

exercises.question: What does 'zorgtoeslag' mean?

options.B: Government healthcare benefit/subsidy for low incomes

exercises.explanation: Zorgtoeslag = healthcare benefit/subsidy. The Dutch government pays zorgtoeslag to people with lower incomes to help cover the cost of health insurance. Zorg = care, toeslag = supplement/allowance.

exercises.question: How do you say 'You can switch health insurance provider' in Dutch?

options.A: U kunt een andere zorgverzekering aanvragen.
options.B: U kunt op een andere verzekering overstappen.

exercises.explanation: From the corpus: 'Als u over uw zorgverzekering niet tevreden bent, kunt u overstappen.' = You can switch. Overstappen = to switch/transfer. Aanvragen = apply for. Annuleren = cancel. Verlengen = extend.
exercises.grammar_focus: civic: overstappen op (to switch to) — zorgverzekering context

exercises.question: Find the error: 'Ik weet niet waar woont hij.'

options.B: Should be: 'Ik weet niet waar hij woont' (verb at end in indirect question)
options.C: Should be: 'Ik weet niet hij woont waar'
options.D: Should be: 'Ik weet niet hoe hij woont'

exercises.explanation: In indirect questions (embedded questions), Dutch uses verb-final order: 'Ik weet niet WAAR HIJ WOONT.' = I don't know where he lives. The question word becomes a subordinating conjunction → verb goes to end.
exercises.grammar_focus: indirect questions: word + subject + verb (at end)

exercises.question: Find the error: 'Hij vraagt of kan ik morgen komen.'

options.B: Should be: 'Hij vraagt of ik morgen kan komen'
options.C: Should be: 'Hij vraagt als ik morgen kan komen'
options.D: Should be: 'Hij vraagt wanneer ik morgen kan komen'

exercises.explanation: 'Of' (whether) introduces an indirect yes/no question → verb goes to end: 'of ik morgen KAN KOMEN.' The conjunction is 'of' (not 'als' for indirect questions). Verb moves to the last position.
exercises.grammar_focus: indirect yes/no question: of + subject + [rest] + verb (at end)

exercises.question: Complete the fixed phrase: 'Dat hangt ______ het weer ______.'
exercises.explanation: 'Dat hangt van... af' = That depends on... From vocabulary: '*Dat hangt van... af* = That depends on...' This is a separable verb: afhangen van (to depend on). The 'af' moves to the end.
exercises.grammar_focus: fixed expression + separable verb: afhangen van (that depends on)

exercises.question: What does 'verder' mean in 'Verder is alles goed'?
exercises.explanation: Verder = furthermore / apart from that / otherwise. From vocabulary: '*verder...* = furthermore...' Used to continue a point or add: 'Verder wil ik zeggen dat...' = Furthermore I want to say that...

exercises.question: What does 'Misschien kunnen ze...' express?
exercises.explanation: Misschien = maybe/perhaps. 'Misschien kunnen ze...' = Maybe they can... From vocabulary. Misschien + modal verb expresses possibility/suggestion. Common in tentative suggestions at A2 level.

exercises.question: Choose the correct conjunction meaning 'although': ______ het duur is, koop ik het toch.
exercises.explanation: Hoewel = although/even though. 'Hoewel het duur is, koop ik het toch.' = Although it's expensive, I'll buy it anyway. Hoewel is subordinating → verb goes to end of its clause.

exercises.question: What is the difference between 'terwijl' and 'hoewel'?

options.D: Terwijl is informal; hoewel is formal

exercises.explanation: Terwijl = while (two things happening at the same time). 'Ik luister muziek terwijl ik kook.' = I listen to music while I cook. Hoewel = although (concession/contrast). Both are subordinating conjunctions (verb to end).

exercises.question: Complete: 'Dinsdag of woensdag, ______.'
exercises.explanation: From the corpus: 'Dinsdag of woensdag, dat maakt mij niet uit.' = Tuesday or Wednesday, it doesn't matter to me. 'Het maakt niet uit' / 'Dat maakt me niet uit' = I don't mind / it doesn't matter.
exercises.grammar_focus: fixed expression: dat maakt me niet uit (I don't mind)

exercises.question: What does 'conditie' mean?
exercises.explanation: De conditie = physical fitness/stamina (in a health context). From corpus: 'Dat is ook goed voor je conditie!' = That's also good for your fitness! Don't confuse with 'conditie' meaning terms/conditions in a contract.

exercises.question: What does 'het openbaar vervoer' specifically include in the Netherlands?
exercises.explanation: Openbaar vervoer = public transport, which in the Netherlands includes: bus, tram, metro, and trein. The OV-chipkaart is used on all of these. Important civic and daily life knowledge.
exercises.grammar_focus: civic vocabulary: openbaar vervoer = bus + tram + metro + trein

exercises.question: You are unhappy with your health insurance and want to change. When is the ONLY time you can switch?

options.B: Only in January (within the first month of the year)

exercises.explanation: From the corpus: 'Halverwege het jaar kunt u dus niet op een andere verzekering overstappen.' = You cannot switch halfway through the year. You can only switch at the start of a new year. Critical civic health insurance knowledge.
exercises.grammar_focus: civic knowledge: zorgverzekering switching — only in January
